nodes.statement_latex:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.
nodes.statement_plain: (i) ε- : ε>0\, >0\, x A:\,|x-c|< ⇒ |f(x)-f(c)|<ε. (ii) Neighbourhood: f(A V_ (c))⊆ V_ε(f(c)). (iii) Sequential: x_n→ c⇒ f(x_n)→ f(c).

statement.plain: (i) ε- : ε>0\, >0\, x A:\,|x-c|< ⇒ |f(x)-f(c)|<ε. (ii) Neighbourhood: f(A V_ (c))⊆ V_ε(f(c)). (iii) Sequential: x_n→ c⇒ f(x_n)→ f(c).

nodes.question:
State the three equivalent forms of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.

question_structured.plain: State the three equivalent forms of continuity of f at c A.

nodes.hint:
(i)$\iff$(ii) is notation only.
  (i)$\iff$(iii) is the continuity analogue of Bartle 4.1.8,
  but sequences may now include $x_n = c$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. (i)$\Rightarrow$(ii): Fix $\varepsilon_0$, get $\delta_0$
from (i). Any $x_0 \in A \cap V_{\delta_0}(c)$ satisfies
$f(x_0) \in V_{\varepsilon_0}(f(c))$; arbitrariness gives the inclusion.
2. (ii)$\Rightarrow$(iii): Fix $\varepsilon$, get $\delta$ from (ii).
Sequential characterisation gives $N$ with $x_n \in V_\delta(c)$ for
$n \geq N$; neighbourhood condition pulls $f(x_n) \in V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
3. (iii)$\Rightarrow$(i): Contrapositive. Failure of (i) at $\varepsilon_0$
yields $(x_n)$ via $\delta = 1/n$ with $x_n \to c$ but
$f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$, contradicting~(iii).

nodes.question: State the Division Algorithm.
nodes.hint: Unique quotient and remainder; remainder lies in $[0,b)$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Two-part direct proof. Existence uses the Well-Ordering Principle on the set $S = \{a - bx \geq 0 \mid x \in \mathbb{Z}\}$: its least element is the remainder $r$, and minimality forces $r < |b|$. Uniqueness is an arithmetic squeeze: subtracting two representations gives $b(q'-q) = r-r'$, and the bound $|r'-r| < |b|$ forces $q' = q$ and then $r' = r$. This is the standard pattern for uniqueness in divisibility arguments — assume two solutions, subtract, use the size bound to eliminate the non-trivial case.
nodes.uses_text:
• Well-Ordering Principle — to guarantee $S$ has a least element.
  • Definition (Divisibility) — context for what $b \mid a$ means when $r = 0$.
  • Absolute value and integer arithmetic — $|b(q'-q)| = |b||q'-q|$ and the bound $|r'-r| < |b|$.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_plain: Let A be a set. enumerate[label=( )] If R is an equivalence relation on A, then A/R is a partition of A. If P is a partition of A, then the relation R_{P} defined by \[ (a,b) R_{P} \; \; P P with a P and b P \] is an equivalence relation on A. These constructions are inverse: R_{A/R} = R and A/R_{P} = P. enumerate

statement.plain: Let A be a set. enumerate[label=( )] If R is an equivalence relation on A, then A/R is a partition of A. If P is a partition of A, then the relation R_{P} defined by \[ (a,b) R_{P} \; \; P P with a P and b P \] is an equivalence relation on A. These constructions are inverse: R_{A/R} = R and A/R_{P} = P. enumerate

nodes.formal_latex:
(i) $\forall a \in A,\; [a]_R \neq \varnothing$; $\forall a,b \in A,\; [a]_R \neq [b]_R \to [a]_R \cap [b]_R = \varnothing$; $\bigcup_{a \in A}[a]_R = A$.\\
(ii) $\forall a,b \in A:\; (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\leftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P},\; a \in P \wedge b \in P$.

nodes.statement_latex:
Suppose
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(c,d)=1,
\quad b,d>0.
\]
If
\[
bc-ad=1,
\]
then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced
form:
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]
nodes.statement_plain: Suppose \[ a{b} 0. \] If \[ bc-ad=1, \] then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced form: \[ a+c{b+d}. \]

There exists $\delta > 0$ such that $P(f(x), …)$
  holds for all $x \in I$ with $0 < |x - x_0| < \delta$.
  Written $P(f(x), …)\ (x \approx x_0)$.
nodes.statement_plain: There exists > 0 such that P(f(x), …) holds for all x I with 0 < |x - x_0| < . Written P(f(x), …)\ (x x_0).

statement.plain: There exists > 0 such that P(f(x), …) holds for all x I with 0 < |x - x_0| < . Written P(f(x), …)\ (x x_0).

nodes.question: Define: property $P$ is true near $x_0$.

question_structured.plain: Define: property P is true near x_0.

nodes.hint:
The $0 <$ excludes $x_0$ itself — the property need
  only hold on the punctured neighbourhood $\dot{B}_\delta(x_0)$.
  The limit definition is the special case $P \equiv
  |f(x) - y_0| < \varepsilon$.
nodes.proof_sketch: $(a)\Rightarrow(b)$: BW gives convergent subseq; closure lands limit in $X$. $(b)\Rightarrow(a)$: not-closed gives seq escaping $X$, subseq limit must be in $X$ (contradiction). Not-bounded gives $|a_n|>n$, subseq converges hence bounded (contradiction).

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $A,B$ be sets and $U$ a universe with $A \subseteq U$. Then
\[
A \cup \varnothing = A,
\qquad
A \cap U = A,
\]
\[
A \cup (A \cap B) = A,
\qquad
A \cap (A \cup B) = A.
\]

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
\item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
\item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
\end{enumerate}
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then
\[
f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A
\quad\text{and}\quad
f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B.
\]
Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.

nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Fix $x$. Induct on $y$.
2. Base: $f(x,1) = x' = g(x,1)$ by A6.
3. Step: $f(x,y') = (f(x,y))' = (g(x,y))' = g(x,y')$ by A7 and IH.
4. Since $x$ arbitrary, $f = g$.

nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Fix $x, y$. Induct on $z$.
2. Base: $(x+y)+1 = (x+y)' = x+y' = x+(y+1)$ by A6 and A7.
3. Step: $(x+y)+z' = ((x+y)+z)' = (x+(y+z))' = x+(y+z)' = x+(y+z')$
   by A7, IH, A7 backwards, A7 backwards.

nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Fix $x$. Induct on $y$.
2. Base: $x + 1 = x' = 1 + x$ by A6 and successor lemma.
3. Step: $x + y' = (x+y)' = (y+x)' = y + x' = y' + x$
   by A7, IH, A7 backwards, A7 backwards.

  Base: $1 + y = y'$ and $1 + z = z'$ by A6; $y \neq z$ gives $y' \neq z'$
  by Theorem~1.
  Step: A7 rewrites $x' + y = (x+y)'$ and $x' + z = (x+z)'$;
  apply Theorem~1 to the IH $x + y \neq x + z$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
1. Fix $y 
eq z$; induct on $x$. \
2. Base: $1+y = y'$, $1+z = z'$ by A6; $y 
eq z$ gives $y' 
eq z'$ by Thm~1. \
3. Step: $x'+y = (x+y)'$, $x'+z = (x+z)'$ by A7; IH gives $x+y 
eq x+z$;
   Thm~1 gives $(x+y)' 
eq (x+z)'$.

  once for each candidate, then reconcile with

nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Assume $L \neq L'$; set $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2 > 0$.
2. Invoke limit definition twice to get $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; set
   $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$.
3. Find $x \in A$ with $0 < |x-c| < \delta$; triangle inequality gives
   $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.

nodes.formal_latex:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]

nodes.question: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?

question_structured.plain: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?

nodes.hint: Continuity on a compact interval forces the extreme values not only to exist as bounds but to be attained.
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Use boundedness to define sup f(I) and inf f(I). 2. Choose sequences whose function values approach these extrema. 3. Extract convergent subsequences in [a,b] and apply continuity to show the extreme values are attained.

nodes.statement_latex:
For all $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \in \mathbb{Z}$:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item[(i)] Commutativity: $\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \beta
    = \beta \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \alpha$.
  \item[(ii)] Associativity: $\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
    (\beta \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \gamma)
    = (\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \beta) \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \gamma$.
  \item[(iii)] Distributivity: $\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
    (\beta +_{\mathbb{Z}} \gamma)
    = (\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \beta)
    +_{\mathbb{Z}} (\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \gamma)$.
  \item[(iv)] Multiplicative identity: $\alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
    1_{\mathbb{Z}} = \alpha$.
  \item[(v)] No zero divisors: $\alpha \neq 0_{\mathbb{Z}} \wedge
    \beta \neq 0_{\mathbb{Z}} \Rightarrow
    \alpha \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \beta \neq 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.
\end{enumerate}

  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.

nodes.question: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?

question_structured.plain: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?

nodes.hint:
Disjoint intervals $\hookrightarrow$ distinct rationals
  $\hookrightarrow$ countable.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing. 2. Each $c \in D$ gives non-empty
$J_c(f) = (f(c^-), f(c^+))$; pick $r(c) \in J_c(f) \cap \mathbb{Q}$ by density.
3. Jump intervals are pairwise disjoint (Jump Intervals corollary), so $r$ is
injective. 4. Injection $D \hookrightarrow \mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Q}$ countable,
so $D$ at most countable.

nodes.statement_latex:
A bounded monotone function on $(a,b)$ has both one-sided
  limits at every interior point. For increasing $f$: $f(x_0^-) =
  \sup\{f(x):x<x_0\}$ and $f(x_0^+) = \inf\{f(x):x>x_0\}$.
nodes.statement_plain: A bounded monotone function on (a,b) has both one-sided limits at every interior point. For increasing f: f(x_0^-) = \{f(x):x x_0\}.

statement.plain: A bounded monotone function on (a,b) has both one-sided limits at every interior point. For increasing f: f(x_0^-) = \{f(x):x x_0\}.

nodes.question:
What are the one-sided limits of an increasing function at
  an interior point $c$?

question_structured.plain: What are the one-sided limits of an increasing function at an interior point c?

nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Set $s = \sup S$, $j = \inf J$; both finite by boundedness from interior
point. 2. For $\lim_{x \to c^{-}}$: $s - \varepsilon$ not an upper bound gives $x_0 
c$; take $\delta = c - x_0$; monotonicity pins $f(x) \in (s-\varepsilon, s]$. 3.
Right-hand limit is the mirror via $\inf J$. 4. Ordering follows from monotonicity
applied to all $x < c$ (resp.\ $x > c$).

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$).
\item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
\item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$
and $f$ is bijective.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_plain: Let f : A → B. enumerate[label=( )] f has a left inverse iff f is injective (and A ≠ or A=B= ). f has a right inverse iff f is surjective. If f has both a left inverse g and a right inverse h, then g = h and f is bijective. enumerate

statement.plain: Let f : A → B. enumerate[label=( )] f has a left inverse iff f is injective (and A ≠ or A=B= ). f has a right inverse iff f is surjective. If f has both a left inverse g and a right inverse h, then g = h and f is bijective. enumerate

(ii) $(\exists h: B\to A,\; \forall b\in B,\; f(h(b))=b) \;\leftrightarrow\; \forall b\in B,\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=b$.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\).
Suppose that
\[
f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c.
\]
Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
\item If
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty.
\]

\item If
\[
\lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty.
\]
\end{enumerate}

Suppose that \[ f(x) g(x) for all x A,\ x c. \] Then: enumerate[label=(\alph*)] If \[ _{x→ c} f(x)=∞, \] then \[ _{x→ c} g(x)=∞. \] If \[ _{x→ c} g(x)=-∞, \] then \[ _{x→ c} f(x)=-∞. \] enumerate

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_plain: Let f : A → B and S, T ⊆ B. Then enumerate[label=( )] f^{-1}(S T) = f^{-1}(S) f^{-1}(T), f^{-1}(S T) = f^{-1}(S) f^{-1}(T), f^{-1}(S T) = f^{-1}(S) f^{-1}(T), f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c. enumerate

nodes.formal_latex:
(i) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S\cup T) \leftrightarrow (f(a)\in S \vee f(a)\in T)$.\\
(ii) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S\cap T) \leftrightarrow (f(a)\in S \wedge f(a)\in T)$.\\
(iv) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S^c) \leftrightarrow f(a)\notin S$.

\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]

nodes.question: What does continuity do to intervals?

question_structured.plain: What does continuity do to intervals?

nodes.hint: This is the conceptual endpoint of IVT: continuous functions do not create gaps in images of intervals.
nodes.proof_sketch:
Take $y_1, y_2 \in f(I)$, $y_1 < k < y_2$. Pull back:
$f(x_1)=y_1$, $f(x_2)=y_2$. $J = [\min(x_1,x_2),\max(x_1,x_2)]
\subseteq I$ since $I$ is an interval. IVT on $J$ gives $c$ with
$f(c)=k$. So $k \in f(I)$. Hence $f(I)$ is closed under betweenness,
i.e., an interval.
nodes.uses_text: Characterization of Intervals, Intermediate Value Theorem

nodes.statement_latex:
Quantifiers of the same type commute:
\begin{align*}
\forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi, \\
\exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi.
\end{align*}
However, quantifiers of different types do \emph{not} commute in general:
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi \;\not\equiv\; \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi.
\]

nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Combine the difference quotient of $f/g$ over a common
  denominator $g(x)g(c)(x-c)$.
  2. Insert $\pm f(c)g(c)$ in the numerator to isolate the difference
  quotients of $f$ and $g$.
  3. Apply limit laws; continuity of $g$ gives $\lim g(x) = g(c)$;
  differentiability gives $f'(c)$ and $g'(c)$.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the
canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is
\emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies
$f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function
$\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that
\[
f = \bar{f} \circ \pi.
\]
When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.
nodes.statement_plain: Let R be an equivalence relation on A, let : A → A/R be the canonical surjection, and let f : A → B be any function. Then f is constant on equivalence classes — meaning (a,b) R implies f(a) = f(b) — if and only if there exists a unique function f : A/R → B such that \[ f = f . \] When it exists, f is defined by f([a]) = f(a).

statement.plain: Let R be an equivalence relation on A, let : A → A/R be the canonical surjection, and let f : A → B be any function. Then f is constant on equivalence classes — meaning (a,b) R implies f(a) = f(b) — if and only if there exists a unique function f : A/R → B such that \[ f = f . \] When it exists, f is defined by f([a]) = f(a).

nodes.formal_latex: $\bigl(\forall a,b \in A,\; (a,b)\in R \to f(a) = f(b)\bigr) \;\leftrightarrow\; \bigl(\exists!\, \bar{f} : A/R \to B,\; \forall a \in A,\; \bar{f}([a]) = f(a)\bigr)$.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty
\qquad
[\text{respectively, } \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty].
\]
\item For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \((a,\infty)\) such that
\[
x_n\to\infty,
\]
we have
\[
f(x_n)\to\infty
\qquad
[\text{respectively, } f(x_n)\to-\infty].
\]
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_plain: Let \(A ⊆ ℝ\), let \(f : A → ℝ\), and suppose that \[ (a,∞)⊆ A \] for some \(a ℝ\). Then the following are equivalent: enumerate[label=(\roman*)] \[ _{x→∞} f(x)=∞ [respectively, _{x→∞} f(x)=-∞]. \] For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \((a,∞)\) such that \[ x_n→∞, \] we have \[ f(x_n)→∞ [respectively, f(x_n)→-∞]. \] enumerate

Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let
$c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (-\infty, c)$.
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L$.
  \item For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that
        \[
          x_n \in A, \qquad x_n < c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c,
        \]
        the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_plain: Let A ⊆ ℝ, let f : A → ℝ, and let c ℝ be a cluster point of A (-∞, c). Then the following are equivalent: enumerate[label=(\roman*)] _{x → c^-} f(x) = L. For every sequence (x_n) such that \[ x_n A, x_n < c for all n, x_n → c, \] the image sequence (f(x_n)) converges to L. enumerate

statement.plain: Let A ⊆ ℝ, let f : A → ℝ, and let c ℝ be a cluster point of A (-∞, c). Then the following are equivalent: enumerate[label=(\roman*)] _{x → c^-} f(x) = L. For every sequence (x_n) such that \[ x_n A, x_n < c for all n, x_n → c, \] the image sequence (f(x_n)) converges to L. enumerate

nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. ($\Rightarrow$) Fix $\varepsilon$; get $\delta$; use $x_n \to c$ and
     $x_n < c$ to find $N_0$ with $0 < c - x_n < \delta$; conclude
     $|f(x_n) - L| < \varepsilon$. \\
  2. ($\Leftarrow$) Negate limit: fix $\varepsilon_0$; for each $n$ pick
     $x_n \in A$ with $0 < c - x_n < 1/n$ and $|f(x_n) - L| \geq
     \varepsilon_0$. \\
  3. Archimedean $N_1 > 1/\varepsilon$ gives $x_n \to c$; sequence witnesses
     failure of the sequential condition.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $\{\mathcal{M}_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ be any family of $\sigma$-algebras
on $X$ (indexed by any set $I$). Then
\[
\mathcal{M} \;:=\; \bigcap_{\alpha \in I} \mathcal{M}_\alpha
\]
is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$.
nodes.statement_plain: Let \{M_ \}_{ I} be any family of -algebras on X (indexed by any set I). Then \[ M \;:=\; _{ I} M_ \] is a -algebra on X.

statement.plain: Let \{M_ \}_{ I} be any family of -algebras on X (indexed by any set I). Then \[ M \;:=\; _{ I} M_ \] is a -algebra on X.

nodes.formal_latex:
$\bigl(\forall\alpha\in I,\; \mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X\bigr) \;\to\; \bigcap_{\alpha\in I}\mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X$.\\
Explicitly: $X\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $A\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to A^c\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $\forall n,\; A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to \bigcup_n A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$.
nodes.formal_plain: ( I,\; M_ is a \sigma-algebra on X ) \;→\; _{ I}M_ is a \sigma-algebra on X.\\ Explicitly: X M_ ; A M_ → A^c M_ ; n,\; A_n M_ → _n A_n M_ .

formal_structured.plain: ( I,\; M_ is a \sigma-algebra on X ) \;→\; _{ I}M_ is a \sigma-algebra on X.\\ Explicitly: X M_ ; A M_ → A^c M_ ; n,\; A_n M_ → _n A_n M_ .

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c$ be a
cluster point of $A$. Suppose that
\[
\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L = \lim_{x \to c} g(x).
\]
If there exists $\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
\forall x \in A,\quad 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0 \implies f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x),
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c} h(x) = L$.

\begin{remark*}[Fully quantified form]
\[
\Bigl(\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c}g(x)\Bigr)
\;\wedge\;
\Bigl(\exists\delta_0>0,\;\forall x\in A,\;
0<|x-c|<\delta_0\implies f(x)\leq h(x)\leq g(x)\Bigr)
\implies \lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L.
\]
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Relationship to sequential version]
This is the function-limit analogue of the Squeeze Theorem for sequences.
The proof routes entirely through the Sequential Criterion: translate to
sequences, apply the sequential squeeze, translate back. No new $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
work is needed.
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Flash]\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Proof link]
Proof: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
\end{remark*}

nodes.statement_latex:
Let

\[
\left(\frac{a_n}{b_n}\right),\quad
\left(\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right)
\]

be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot
refinement so that

\[
\frac{a_n}{b_n}
<
\frac{c_n}{d_n}
\]

and

\[
\left[\frac{a_{n+1}}{b_{n+1}},\frac{c_{n+1}}{d_{n+1}}\right]
\subseteq
\left[\frac{a_n}{b_n},\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right].
\]

Then there exists a unique real number

\[
x
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]
nodes.statement_plain: Let \[ (a_n{b_n} ), (c_n{d_n} ) \] be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot refinement so that \[ a_n{b_n} < c_n{d_n} \] and \[ ⊆ . \] Then there exists a unique real number \[ x = _{n→∞}a_n{b_n} = _{n→∞}c_n{d_n}. \]

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $x>0$ be a real number.  Suppose one begins with two rational
numbers

\[
\frac{a}{b} < x < \frac{c}{d},
\qquad b,d>0,
\]

and repeatedly inserts the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new
interval still contains $x$.  Then this process generates a nested
sequence of rational intervals containing $x$.
nodes.statement_plain: Let x>0 be a real number. Suppose one begins with two rational numbers \[ a{b} 0, \] and repeatedly inserts the mediant \[ a+c{b+d}. \] At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new interval still contains x. Then this process generates a nested sequence of rational intervals containing x.

Then this process generates a nested sequence of rational intervals containing x.

nodes.statement_latex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$
be a cluster point of both
\[
A \cap (c,\infty)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
A \cap (-\infty,c).
\]
Then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x).
\]
nodes.statement_plain: Let A ⊆ ℝ, let f : A → ℝ, and let c ℝ be a cluster point of both \[ A (c,∞) and A (-∞,c). \] Then \[ _{x→ c} f(x)=L _{x→ c^+} f(x)=L= _{x→ c^-} f(x). \]

statement.plain: Let A ⊆ ℝ, let f : A → ℝ, and let c ℝ be a cluster point of both \[ A (c,∞) and A (-∞,c). \] Then \[ _{x→ c} f(x)=L _{x→ c^+} f(x)=L= _{x→ c^-} f(x). \]

nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. $(\Rightarrow)$: two-sided $\delta$ restricts to each side directly.\\
  2. $(\Leftarrow)$: set $\delta = \min\{\delta_+, \delta_-\}$.\\
  3. Split on $x > c$ and $x < c$; apply respective one-sided hypothesis.\\
  4. Conclude $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$ in both cases.

nodes.negation_latex:
The set of Lipschitz functions with a \emph{fixed}
  constant $K$ is also convex (the constant of the combination
  is at most $K$), but the set of all Lipschitz functions
  (varying constants) is a vector space, not just convex.
nodes.negation_plain: The set of Lipschitz functions with a fixed constant K is also convex (the constant of the combination is at most K), but the set of all Lipschitz functions (varying constants) is a vector space, not just convex.

nodes.question: Is the set of uniformly continuous functions on $A$ convex?

question_structured.plain: Is the set of uniformly continuous functions on A convex?

nodes.hint:
Convex combination is a linear combination with
  non-negative coefficients summing to 1. The algebra theorem
  gives UC for any linear combination, so convexity is immediate.
